unclustered.note: Natural heap order — random IO for region predicate

clustered.note: After CLUSTER — sequential IO for region predicate

postgres.demonstrates: Physical clustering provides 6.7x speedup for range predicates

validation.concept: Physical sort order reduces random I/O via zone-map / clustered heap
validation.proof: Unclustered: 38.14s → Clustered: 5.73s (6.7x). IO-bound dropped from 77.9% to 31.6%.
validation.validates: Article 3: CLUSTER command enables sequential I/O for range predicates
validation.interpretation: Before CLUSTER, Postgres scattered rows across heap pages in insertion order — each region predicate required random reads across the entire 50M-row table (77.9% IO-bound). After CLUSTER BY region, matching rows are physically adjacent, enabling sequential I/O and a 6.7x speedup. IO-bound fraction dropped to 31.6%, validating Lecture 04 storage model theory.

unsorted.note: Random region distribution per row group — zone maps provide no benefit

sorted.note: Sorted Parquet — zone map stats skip 75% of row groups

duckdb.demonstrates: Zone map pruning: 6.6x speedup from sort-order clustering

validation.concept: Parquet row group statistics enable predicate pushdown without CLUSTER
validation.proof: Unsorted: 12.87s → Zone-mapped: 1.94s (6.6x). IO-bound dropped from 38.7% to 10.9%.
validation.validates: Article 3: Zone maps skip 75% of row groups for selective predicates
validation.interpretation: DuckDB stores min/max statistics per Parquet row group. On unsorted data, every row group may contain any region, so all must be scanned. After sorting by region, each row group contains a narrow range — DuckDB's zone map skips 75% of row groups entirely, reducing peak memory from 920 MB to 210 MB and achieving a 6.6x speedup without any explicit CLUSTER command.
